288 East 10th Street | Red Brick Gospel

MLA BIBLIOGRAPHY & LYRICAL PHRASE EXPLANATIONS


(Ordered by appearance in lyrics)


[8] "Two-eight-eight East Tenth, where the free spirits breathe" → Inspired by the neighborhood description: "The impressive 1883 redbrick Gothic Revival building was once an outreach chapel of St. Mark's Church-in-the-Bowery (Episcopal)." Swacker. (2019) Out From Midtown, p. 109.


[10] "Eighteen-eighty-three red brick rising from the leaves" → Directly anchored in: "Was

built in 1883." Unknown Author. Landmarks Preservation Commission, p. 1.


[7] "Gothic Revival glory, Renaissance dreams in the mortar" → Drawn from the architectural description: "Architecture Style: Gothic-Revival, Renaissance-Revival... Façade Material: Brick, Terra-cotta, Brownstone." LPC. (1883) Tompkins Square Park, p. 1.


[15] "Renwick laid the corner, Stuyvesant blood gave the order" → Pulled from: "In 1881, Rutherford Stuyvesant, a descendant of former Dutch Governor Peter Stuyvesant, constructed the Memorial Chapel of St. Mark's Parish... Designed by one of the foremost architects of the Gothic Revival style." Spielvogel. (2016) The Landmarks of New York, p. 301.


[22] "Red brick gospel on Avenue A" → Drawn from: "Gothic Revival in exuberant red brick and matching terra-cotta." AIA. (1988) AIA Edition 3, p. 163.


[3] "Saint Marks Memorial — they couldn't make it stay" → Referenced from: "In the 1897 Bromleys Atlas, on 155 Avenue A, there is written 'Saint Marks Memorial Chapel.'" Bromley. 1897 Bromleys Atlas, p. 17.


[12] "Carpatho-Russian faithful came and claimed the nave" → Drawn from: "this building was occupied in 1925 by the newly formed St. Nicholas of Myra Church of the American Carpatho-Russian Orthodox Diocese. It rented the building until 1937 and then bought it." Dunlap. (2004) From Abyssinian to Zion, p. 234.

[15] "Terra-cotta lion watch over every soul they saved" → Echoes: "A bas-relief lion on the façade testifies to its origins under the patronage of St. Mark." Spielvogel. (2016) The Landmarks of New York, p. 301.


[16] "Slovak congregation held the walls for a spell" → Pulled from: "Holy Trinity Slovak Lutheran Church, which moved here in 1911 from 288 East 10th Street." Dunlap. (2004) From Abyssinian to Zion, p. 105.


[16] "Holy Trinity passed the torch — they know it well" → Directly references: "the building on this site served the... Holy Trinity Slovak Lutheran Church, which moved here in 1911 from 288 East 10th Street." Dunlap. (2004) From Abyssinian to Zion, p. 105.


[12] "Nineteen-twenty-five they rented, thirty-seven they bought" → Drawn from: "It rented

the building until 1937 and then bought it." Dunlap. (2004) From Abyssinian to Zion, p. 234.


[12] "English and Church Slavonic — every sermon hard-fought" → Directly quoted from: "Services are conducted in English and Church Slavonic." Dunlap. (2004) From Abyssinian to Zion, p. 234.


[7] "Pointed arch windows, campanile piercing the sky" → Drawn from the architectural inventory: "Window Detail: Pointed Arched Lintels... Roofline Description: Campanile w/ Peaked Roof." LPC. (1883) Tompkins Square Park, p. 1.


[15] "A bas-relief lion standing proud and standing high" → Directly from: "A bas-relief lion on the façade testifies to its origins under the patronage of St. Mark." Spielvogel. (2016) The Landmarks of New York, p. 301.


[12] "Christmas comes in January on this sacred ground" → Verbatim inspiration from: "And

Christmas comes in January." Dunlap. (2004) From Abyssinian to Zion, p. 234.


[20] "Five crystal chandeliers spinning holy light around" → Pulled directly from: "Five crystal chandeliers add glorious light to the renovated sanctuary with iconostasis." Cook. (2001) Sacred Havens, p. 55.


[22] "Two-eight-eight East Tenth — Manhattan's layered lore" → Closing anchor drawn from: "288 E. 10th St., SW cor. Avenue A. 1883. James Renwick, Jr. and W. H. Russell." AIA. (1988) AIA Edition 3, p. 163.

[8] "Iconostasis rising — they unlocked a sacred door" → Drawn from: "It has a beautiful new iconostasis (icon screen) inside partially separating the sanctuary from the nave." Swacker. (2019) Out From Midtown, p. 109.
